name: The Henry Ford, Dearborn
concept&appearance: The museum's architectural design is centered on a full-scale replica of Independence Hall, Congress Hall, and Old City Hall in Philadelphia, which form the main facade spanning 800 feet. Behind this historic colonial facade lies a single, massive 523,000-square-foot exhibit hall designed by architect Robert O. Derrick. Unlike the multi-story proposal originally submitted, Henry Ford insisted on a single-level structure to house the collections, emphasizing a 'learning by doing' philosophy. The interior features an iconic teak floor laid in a herringbone pattern, which covers nearly the entire exhibition space. The building serves as a 'garage' for America's history, combining the grandeur of 18th-century architecture with the functional scale of a 20th-century factory floor.
permanent_exhibitions: The museum houses several major permanent exhibitions: 1. 'Driving America': The world's premier automotive exhibition, featuring over 100 vehicles (including the 1896 Ford Quadricycle) and examining the car's influence on American culture. 2. 'Heroes of the Sky': An aviation exhibit documenting the first 40 years of flight, including a replica of the Wright Flyer and the Fokker F.VII 'Josephine Ford' flown by Admiral Byrd. 3. 'With Liberty and Justice for All': A profound exhibit exploring the evolution of American freedom, featuring the actual bus where Rosa Parks refused to give up her seat and the chair Abraham Lincoln was sitting in when he was assassinated. 4. 'Made in America': Focuses on the legacy of American manufacturing and power, showcasing steam engines and industrial machinery. 5. 'Presidential Vehicles': A collection of presidential limousines, including the 1961 Lincoln Continental in which John F. Kennedy was assassinated. 6. 'Mathematica': An interactive exhibit originally designed by Charles and Ray Eames to help visitors visualize mathematical concepts. 7. 'Your Place in Time': Explores 20th-century generational culture and technology.
central_hall: The museum's interior is dominated by the 'Great Hall' (or Main Exhibit Hall), a vast, open 7-acre space featuring a high ceiling and the signature herringbone teak flooring. This open-plan design allows for the display of massive artifacts, such as steam locomotives (like the Allegheny locomotive) and airplanes suspended from the ceiling, creating an immersive industrial atmosphere.
special_effects_theaters: Giant Screen Experience: Formerly an IMAX theater, this venue was upgraded in 2016 to a state-of-the-art 4K digital projection system with a screen measuring 80 feet wide by 42 feet tall. It screens educational documentaries (often 3D) and classic feature films.
science_popularization_activities: The museum is deeply committed to 'learning by doing' educational philosophies. Key activities include: 1. 'Invention Convention': A global K-12 invention education program and competition. 2. Summer Camps: Hands-on programs for grades 2-9 exploring innovation and history. 3. 'Henry Ford Academy': A charter high school located directly within the museum complex where students use the artifacts for learning. 4. Interactive science and maker programs held in connection with exhibitions like 'Mathematica' and the 'Tinker. Hack. Invent.' series.
